data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-06T01:37:06.772Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core and the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about .NET versioning. Let me think about what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, there has been a progression of changes regarding synchronous API support:

1. In EF Core 9 (with .NET 9), the Cosmos DB provider started throwing exceptions for synchronous operations by default, but provided an opt-in to allow sync-over-async behavior.

2. The opt-in was via `CosmosOptionsExtension` or similar configuration that allowed synchronous operations to work by internally calling async methods synchronously.


3. In subsequent versions, the plan was to phase out this opt-in entirely, moving toward async-only APIs for the Cosmos provider. The timeline likely had the opt-in deprecated in EF Core 10 and potentially removed by EF Core 11, though I should verify the exact version where the removal happens.

Actually, let me reconsider the timeline more carefully. Since .NET 9 shipped in November 2024 with the Cosmos provider dropping sync support and introducing the opt-in flag, and we're now in May 2026, .NET 11 would be in preview. The plan was probably to keep the opt-in available through at least one more release before removing it entirely. The Cosmos SDK doesn't support synchronous operations natively, so EF Core was using sync-over-async which can cause deadlocks. In EF Core 9, they blocked sync operations by default but allowed opting back in through the Cosmos options builder. For .NET 11, removing that opt-in means all synchronous calls will fail and code must migrate to async patterns throughout.
